elements.rationale: Section 1 explicitly states intended use, business decisions supported, scope of appropriate use, and an itemized list of must-not-be-used-for cases.
Section 2 derives the BSM PDE, gives closed-form call/put formulas with d1/d2, lists analytical Greeks, and cites the numerical CDF approximation.
Section 3 names and explains seven assumptions (log-normal returns, constant volatility, no dividends, frictionless markets, continuous trading, constant rate, no arbitrage).
Section 4 maps each assumption to its breakdown conditions with quantitative thresholds (e.g., strikes >15% from spot, bid-ask >5%).
Section 5 names every input (S, K, T, sigma, r, q), source vendor (Bloomberg, OPRA, Treasury curve), and explicit data quality / staleness rules.
Section 6 defines numeric applicability boundaries (moneyness 70-130%, maturity 1d-2y, ADV >500k) and lists explicit not-appropriate-for cases.
Section 7 proactively discloses five named weaknesses with quantitative impact (e.g., tail-risk underestimated 2.7x, dividend errors >2% premium).
Section 8 specifies a 3-year rolling window (Jan 2022-Dec 2024, 756 days), daily frequency, three measured dimensions, and reporting cadence.
Section 9 defines six metrics (RMSE, MAE, hit rate, hedge effectiveness ratio, VaR exception rate, ADF on residuals) and reports concrete values for each.
Section 10 benchmarks against SABR (RMSE 0.019 vs 0.023) and a historical-vol model (0.041), with quantitative comparisons.
Section 11 quantitatively shocks volatility, rates, spot, and time and reports resulting price changes for an ATM call.
Section 12 defines and reports results for three named stress scenarios (2008 crisis, 2010 flash crash, 80% vol spike).
Section 13 documents a 5-year training / 12-month hold-out split with explicit RMSE, MAE, hit-rate, and VaR exception comparisons.
Section 14 reports Kupiec POF, Christoffersen independence and conditional coverage, Jarque-Bera, Ljung-Box, and Durbin-Watson with test statistics and p-values.
Section 15 lists five named KPIs with explicit numeric thresholds, current values, and breach actions.
Section 16 specifies daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual activities with named tasks at each frequency.
Section 17 defines four escalation levels with specific roles, timelines (1 hour, 24 hours, 5 business days), and audit-trail logging in ServiceNow.
Section 18 enumerates seven specific quantitative and event-based triggers for model review.
Section 19 details completeness, timeliness (specific staleness windows), outlier (5-sigma), consistency, and historical integrity controls.
Section 20 defines a six-step change process with classification tiers, parallel-run windows, validation sign-off, and emergency procedures.
Document has no Conceptual Soundness section; model purpose and intended use are never stated.
No theoretical or mathematical framework is documented. Logistic regression and scorecard methodology are referenced only in passing within OA sections.
No assumptions are listed or explained anywhere in the document.
No limitations or boundary conditions for assumptions are discussed (the doc has no assumptions to bound).
Although input variables (DTI, FICO, etc.) are referenced in OA sections, the document never documents data inputs and sources as a CS element.
Scope is implied through portfolio mentions (retail credit exposures) but never formally documented as approved/not-approved use cases.
No proactive disclosure of known weaknesses or failure modes.
Section 1 (Backtesting Methodology) specifies a 5-year rolling window 2019-2024, quarterly frequency, 12-month forward window, and the three evaluation dimensions.
Section 2 defines and reports values for Gini (0.72), KS (0.48), PSI (0.08), Hosmer-Lemeshow chi-sq + p-value, Accuracy Ratio, and default rate by score band.
Section 3 benchmarks against logistic regression (0.69), FICO (0.65), and Random Forest (0.76) with explicit Gini comparisons.
Section 4 quantitatively reports PD changes for DTI, payment history, utilization, credit history, and inquiries, plus a cross-variable interaction.
Section 5 defines baseline, adverse, severely adverse, and reverse stress scenarios with reported PD and loss outcomes for each.
Section 6 documents a temporal hold-out (2015-2021 dev / 2022-2024 test) and geographic hold-out (5 states), with Gini, KS, PSI, calibration p-value reported.
Section 7 reports binomial test, chi-squared goodness-of-fit, Spearman rank correlation, likelihood ratio test, and VIF with statistics and p-values.
Five named KPIs with explicit thresholds (Gini >0.60, PSI <0.25, etc.), current values, and tiered breach actions.
Monthly / quarterly / semi-annually / annually activities are individually enumerated.
Four escalation tiers with explicit roles, timelines (4 hours, 5 business days, 3 business days, 1 business day), and decision criteria.
Seven enumerated triggers including Gini drop, calibration failure, PSI threshold, regulatory and macro regime triggers.
Completeness, timeliness, accuracy, and outlier detection are documented with specific tolerances (e.g., <0.5% missing, 99.97% completeness).
Minor / moderate / material / emergency change tiers with parallel-run requirements (60d, 90d) and approval chains.
Section 1 specifies prepayment forecasting purpose, three named business uses (IRR, MBS valuation, ALM), and explicit not-to-be-used-for cases.
Section 2 specifies a logistic + Cox proportional hazards framework with full equations, covariate list, and estimation method (MLE via R).
Section 3 lists six named assumptions with explanations (rational refinancing, observable heterogeneity, stable relationship, HPI patterns, default-prepay independence, proportional hazards).
Section 4 maps each assumption to its breakdown conditions (fintech-driven incentive shifts, COVID forbearance, geographic concentration, rate path dependence, extreme rates).
Section 5 names every input, source (LOS, Black Knight MSP, Freddie PMMS, FHFA HPI), refresh cadence, and validation rules.
Section 6 defines loan types (30y/15y FRM conforming), vintage cutoff (>=2010), portfolio scope, and explicit not-applicable cases.
Section 7 lists five named weaknesses (media effect, burnout heterogeneity, seasonal instability, non-refi prepayments, parameter stability).
Document has no Outcomes Analysis section; backtesting methodology is never described. CPR forecast error is referenced as a KPI in OM-01 but no backtesting framework is documented.
No dedicated performance-metrics section. Metric thresholds (MAPE, AUC, calibration ratio) appear only as forward-looking KPI thresholds in OM-01, not as reported backtested values.

No benchmarking against alternative models documented. Quarterly benchmarking 'against dealer prepayment models (JPM, GS)' is mentioned only as a future monitoring activity, not as a current performance comparison.
No sensitivity analysis section. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, even a qualitative review-statement is Major; here the element is wholly absent.
No stress scenarios defined or stress test results reported.
No train/test split, hold-out period, or out-of-sample results documented.
No formal statistical tests reported (no Kupiec, Hosmer-Lemeshow, or other test statistics with p-values).
Five named KPIs with explicit numeric thresholds and current values (CPR MAPE <20%, AUC >0.75, calibration ratio 0.85-1.15, parameter stability, PSI <0.25).
Monthly / quarterly / semi-annually / annually activities individually enumerated with specific tasks.
Four escalation levels with specific triggers (MAPE >20%, AUC <0.75) and timelines (5/3 business days).
Eight specific triggers documented including sustained MAPE breach, AUC degradation, PSI threshold, rate-regime change, regulatory change.
Loan-level completeness (99.97%), market data timeliness, monthly reconciliation (0.02% discrepancy), and vintage drift detection all documented with thresholds.
Three categories (A/B/C) with parallel-run windows (60d, 90d), independent validation requirements, and emergency procedures.
Section 1 documents intended use, four named business decisions, approved swap types/currencies/maturities, and explicit not-to-be-used-for cases.
Section 2 derives DCF valuation under a multi-curve framework, gives fixed/floating leg formulas, forward rate construction, and bootstrap methodology.
Section 3 lists six named assumptions with explanations (no default risk under CSA, deterministic rates, continuous compounding, perfect collateralization, static notional, day-count accuracy).
Section 4 quantifies breakdown conditions (1-3bp convexity error on 30y, 0.5-2bp uncertainty for non-CSA portfolio, SOFR transition basis).
Section 5 names every input, source (NY Fed SOFR, CME futures, Bloomberg ICAP, Murex, DTCC), validation thresholds, and reconciliation requirements.
Section 6 defines plain-vanilla scope, currencies (USD/EUR/GBP/JPY), maturities (1m-30y), and explicit not-applicable boundary cases.
Section 7 lists five named weaknesses (no convexity adjustment, interpolation sensitivity, end-of-day timing, basis risk, calendar maintenance).
Section 1 (OA) specifies hypothetical-portfolio approach, daily frequency, 3-year window, three measured dimensions, and reporting cadence.
Section 2 (OA) defines and reports five metrics with values: Valuation RMSE 0.42bp, P&L explanation 94.3%, DV01 ratio 0.98, curve fit MAE 0.08bp, bid-ask coverage 97.2%.
Section 3 (OA) benchmarks against LCH/CME (0.35bp), Bloomberg SWPM (0.52bp), and QuantLib (0.12bp) with quantitative comparisons.
Section 4 (OA) reports parallel rate shifts, key rate DV01s by tenor, curve twist, and basis spread sensitivity with quantitative results.
Section 5 (OA) defines and reports three stress scenarios (2022 hike, 2020 COVID, 2s10s inversion) with portfolio impacts and reverse stress test.
Section 6 (OA) documents temporal hold-out (2019-2022 dev / 2023-2024 test) and currency hold-out (EUR) with explicit metric comparisons.
Section 7 (OA) reports residual t-test, Jarque-Bera, Ljung-Box, regression slope, ADF stationarity test, and chi-squared with statistics and p-values.
Document has no Ongoing Monitoring section; no KPIs or thresholds defined for ongoing monitoring.
No monitoring frequency documented anywhere in the document.
No escalation procedures documented.
No trigger conditions for model review documented.
Data quality requirements appear in CS-05 (Data Inputs and Sources) but no ongoing data quality monitoring framework is defined.
No change management process documented.
Document is a single paragraph. 'Various business purposes' is not a documented intended use; no scope of appropriate use stated.
'Advanced techniques' with no equations, methods, or framework name.
No assumptions listed.
No assumption limitations documented (the doc has no assumptions).
No data inputs or sources mentioned.
'Suitable for its intended applications' is a self-assertion, not a documented scope or boundary.
No weaknesses or limitations disclosed; document is purely promotional.
'Tested and found to perform well' is not a backtesting methodology.
No metrics defined or values reported.
'Internal benchmarks' is mentioned but no benchmark model named or compared.
No sensitivity analysis discussed.
No stress scenarios or stress test results.
No train/test split or out-of-sample testing documented.
No formal statistical tests, statistics, or p-values reported.
'Will continue to monitor' with no KPIs or thresholds.
No monitoring frequency documented.
No escalation procedures documented.
No trigger conditions documented.
No data quality monitoring documented.
No change management documented.
Section 1 specifies VaR/options-pricing/RWA uses, approved underlyings, and explicit not-to-be-used-for cases. Embedded injection attempts do not affect substantive coverage.
Section 2 specifies GARCH(1,1) per Bollerslev (1986) with full variance equation, MLE log-likelihood, BFGS optimizer, and multi-step recursion formula.

Five named assumptions with explanations (normal innovations, covariance stationarity, symmetric response, single-asset framework, constant parameters). Injection text appearing between assumption 2 and 3 is data, not part of the documentation.
Section 4 quantifies breakdown conditions (kurtosis 4-8, asymmetric vol response 15% vs 8%, parameter range 0.93-0.99, regime lag 5-10 days).
Section 5 names Bloomberg PX_LAST primary, Refinitiv fallback, returns calc, 504-day window, and three named DQ checks. Injection paragraph in middle is data.
Section 6 defines asset types, forecast horizons (1-day primary, 10-day secondary), markets (NYSE, NASDAQ, etc.), minimum history, and explicit not-applicable cases.
Section 7 lists four named weaknesses (fat-tail underestimation, no leverage, slow regime adaptation, multi-step degradation) with quantitative impact.
Section 1 (OA) specifies rolling 3-year window, 756 trading days, two named frameworks (VaR backtest, vol forecast accuracy), universe (S&P 500 + 50 constituents).
Section 2 (OA) defines and reports 95%/99% VaR exception rates, RMSE 3.2pp, Mincer-Zarnowitz slope/intercept/R², QLIKE 0.043 vs benchmark.
Section 3 (OA) benchmarks against historical vol (RMSE 4.8pp), EWMA (3.5pp), and GJR-GARCH (2.9pp) with QLIKE comparisons.

Section 4 (OA) reports estimation window length, starting values, and distribution-assumption sensitivity with quantitative parameter and metric impacts. Embedded HTML-comment injection is data, not content.
Section 5 (OA) defines and reports three named stress scenarios (COVID-19 crash, flash crash, prolonged low-vol 2017) with model behavior detailed for each.
Section 6 (OA) documents temporal hold-out (2018-2022 / 2023-2024) and cross-asset hold-out (NASDAQ-100) with metric comparisons.
Section 7 (OA) reports Kupiec POF (95% and 99%), Christoffersen Independence, two Ljung-Box, and Jarque-Bera with statistics and p-values.
Section 1 (OM) lists five named KPIs with explicit numeric thresholds and current values (99% VaR <2.5%, RMSE <4.5pp, persistence 0.90-0.995, etc.).
Daily / weekly / monthly / quarterly / annually activities individually enumerated.
Four escalation levels with explicit triggers, roles, and timelines (2 hours, 24 hours, 3 / 5 business days).
Six named triggers (VaR red zone, sustained RMSE, parameter non-stationarity, market regime change, regulatory change, annual cadence).
Section 5 (OM) details price-data completeness, return outlier monitoring, corporate-action handling, and estimation-window integrity.
Three change tiers (minor / moderate / material) with parallel-run windows (30d, 90d), independent validation, and emergency procedures.

Section 1 lists business decisions categorically ('credit origination, portfolio management, loss forecasting, capital allocation, stress testing') but never specifies what the model does, what decisions it supports concretely, or what it should NOT be used for.

Mentions 'machine learning', 'ensemble methods', 'feature engineering', 'regularization' as framework names but provides no equations, no specific algorithm, no derivation. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, framework name without explanation is Major.
Section 3 names six generic assumptions (stationarity, representativeness, data quality, environmental, observable variables, regulatory) but explanations are tautological. Not model-specific.
Discusses limitations in generic terms ('may be violated during economic disruption') without quantitative breakdown thresholds or concrete failure conditions.

Names data source categories ('credit bureau data, public records, economic indicators') but no specific vendors, fields, refresh cadences, or quality requirements. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, this is Major.
Applicability described as 'broad scope of credit products and borrower segments' with no specific product list or boundaries. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, vague applicability is Major.
Lists generic weaknesses (data dependency, complexity, bias, performance degradation) without quantitative impact or model-specific failure modes.
Mentions 'comprehensive backtesting framework' and 'multi-faceted approach' but no specific methodology, period, frequency, or sample. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, this is Major.

References 'comprehensive suite of industry-standard metrics' covering 'predictive accuracy, discrimination power, calibration quality' but no specific metrics named (no AUC, KS, RMSE, etc.) and no values reported. Borderline Critical/Major; the section heading exists so we treat as Major.
Mentions 'simpler statistical models, alternative ML approaches, and industry-standard vendor solutions' as comparison categories but no specific benchmarks named or results reported.
Sensitivity analysis described qualitatively ('local and global techniques') with no quantitative results. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, this is Major.
Mentions sensitivity-based and scenario-based stress tests but no specific scenarios defined or results reported.
Names approaches (temporal hold-out, k-fold CV, segment hold-out) but no specific train/test split, hold-out period, or results.
References a 'battery of complementary tests' and 'rigorous quantitative techniques' but no specific tests named, no statistics, no p-values. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, this is Major.

References 'comprehensive set of KPIs' covering categorical dimensions but no specific KPIs named and no numeric thresholds. Borderline Critical (per AGENT_PROMPTS rule on vague monitoring) but element heading exists, so Major.
Frequencies described categorically ('high-frequency daily-to-weekly', 'medium-frequency monthly-to-quarterly') without specific schedules or activities pinned to each.
Mentions 'multiple tiers of response' but no specific people, timelines, or trigger criteria. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, vague escalation is Major.
Mentions 'performance-based' and 'event-based' trigger categories but no specific triggers documented (no thresholds, no named events).
Describes 'automated quality checks' and 'comprehensive reconciliation' framework but no specific checks, tolerances, or completeness rates. Per AGENT_PROMPTS rule, this is Major.
Describes change-management 'framework' with risk-tiered governance but no specific procedures, parallel-run windows, or approval roles.
Section 1 specifies CECL ACL purpose under ASC 326-20, five named business decisions, six approved CRE segments, and seven explicit not-to-be-used-for cases.
Section 2 derives the PD/LGD/EAD ECL formula and details cloglog hazard PD model, two-stage LGD (multinomial + beta regression), CCF EAD, and prepayment module.
Section 3 lists eight named assumptions with explanations (stationarity, homogeneity, conditional independence, representativeness, R&S horizon, prepayment predictability, LGD timing, discount rate).
Section 4 maps each assumption to specific breakdown conditions plus four numeric override thresholds (unemployment >10%, CRE PI -40%, default rate >3x, rate move >300bp/12mo).
Section 5 names every input, source (LoanIQ, CoStar, BLS, RCA CPPI, Fed H.15, ICE BofA, BEA, Moody's), refresh cadence, and validation rules.
Section 6 specifies 1,847-loan portfolio, six property types with concentrations, geographic distribution, five enumerated boundary conditions.
Section 7 lists six named weaknesses (sponsor concentration, structural shifts in office, GFC overweight, vintage grouping, climate risk gap, linear reversion).
Section 8 specifies three backtesting methodologies (PD binomial test, LGD MAE/RMSE, ECL coverage) with frequencies, samples, acceptance thresholds, and Q4 2025 results.
Section 9 defines and reports AUC 0.78, KS 0.42, Brier 0.037, Hosmer-Lemeshow chi-sq + p, PSI 0.06, plus LGD MAE/RMSE/R² and ECL coverage with targets and current values.
Section 10 benchmarks against vintage loss rate ($378M), Moody's CRE EL ($445M), and Y-14 peer median (2.75% vs institution 2.90%), with quantitative comparisons.
Section 11 reports a sensitivity table for 10 inputs with ECL impact in dollars and percent, plus identifies the four most material drivers.
Section 12 defines and reports three stress scenarios (mild recession, severe recession, CRE-specific) with stressed ECL and CET1 capital impact.
Section 13 documents temporal holdout (12-quarter), 5-fold CV, and external ACLI sample comparison with explicit metric values.
Section 14 reports coefficient SEs and signs for 14 covariates, pseudo-R², LR test, AIC, VIFs, Pregibon link test, and beta-regression dispersion phi.
Section 15 provides a Green/Amber/Red KPI table with seven named KPIs and explicit thresholds plus rationale for each threshold.
Section 16 enumerates monthly / quarterly / semi-annually / annually / ad-hoc activities with specific tasks at each frequency.
Section 17 defines Green->Amber and Amber->Red workflows with named roles, business-day timelines, decision options, and MRMIS audit logging.
Section 18 enumerates quantitative, environmental, and methodological triggers with explicit thresholds (AUC <0.65, coverage 0.60-2.00, PSI 0.25, etc.).
Section 19 details four pipeline stages of automated/manual controls with completeness >99%, outlier thresholds, and lineage/governance.
Section 20 defines three change tiers with explicit examples, seven-step workflow, MRMIS logging, Git version control, 7-year retention, and immutable audit trail.
